algorithms.name: Coin Change (Min Coins)
algorithms.analogy: Find the fewest bills to make change — try every denomination, pick the minimum.
algorithms.story: The cashier problem: you have coins of denominations [1, 5, 10, 25] and need to make $0.41 with the fewest coins. Greedy fails for arbitrary denominations (e.g. [1, 3, 4] making 6 — greedy picks 4+1+1=3 coins, DP finds 3+3=2 coins). DP fills a table: dp[i] = minimum coins to make amount i. For each amount, try every coin: dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i - coin] + 1). This is the canonical unbounded knapsack variant.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Minimum number of items to reach a target (classic framing)
• Coin denominations, step sizes, jump sizes — any 'reach target with min steps' problem
• Unbounded use of each item (coin can be reused)
• Any problem that reduces to: dp[i] = min over all choices c: dp[i - c] + cost(c)
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Create dp array of size amount+1, initialise all to amount+1 (infinity sentinel)
2. Set dp[0] = 0 (base case: 0 coins to make amount 0)
3. For each amount i from 1 to amount:
   For each coin denomination:
     If coin <= i and dp[i - coin] + 1 < dp[i]:
       dp[i] = dp[i - coin] + 1
4. Return dp[amount] if it's not infinity, else return -1
algorithms.interviewTips:
• First ask: greedy doesn't always work — give the [1,3,4] amount=6 counterexample
• State definition clarity: dp[i] = min coins to make exactly amount i
• The inner loop order doesn't matter here (unbounded — coins reusable). Contrast with 0/1 knapsack where order matters
• Follow-up: count the number of ways to make change (dp[i] += dp[i-coin]) instead of minimum
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"ATM Cash Dispensing","desc":"ATM selects minimum number of bills (denominations: 100, 500, 1000, 2000) to dispense the requested amount."},{"title":"Jump Game","desc":"Minimum jumps to reach end of array is structurally identical to coin change — each jump size is a 'coin denomination'."},{"title":"Word Break","desc":"Can a string be broken into dictionary words? Same DP structure: dp[i] = true if dp[i-len(word)] is true for any word."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Using greedy: [1,3,4] coins, amount=6 — greedy picks 4+1+1=3 coins but optimal is 3+3=2","Initialising dp with 0 instead of infinity: dp[i] will never correctly track 'impossible' states","Off-by-one: dp array must have size amount+1 to hold dp[0] through dp[amount]","Confusing coin change (unbounded) with 0/1 knapsack — here each coin can be used unlimited times"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Coin Change 2 (Count Ways)","desc":"Count the number of distinct ways to make the amount — change min() to += in the DP recurrence"},{"name":"Minimum Steps","desc":"Same structure applied to jump games, climbing stairs with variable step sizes, or word break problems"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: dp[i] = min coins for amount i. Greedy FAILS for arbitrary coins ([1,3,4] making 6: greedy=3 coins, DP=2 coins — this is the canonical counterexample). Initialize with amount+1 as infinity sentinel (not Integer.MAX_VALUE to avoid overflow on +1). This is the canonical UNBOUNDED knapsack variant.
algorithms.patternSignal: minimum coins, reach target amount, minimum steps, unbounded knapsack, reuse elements, word break minimum, perfect squares minimum
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Coin Change","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"dp[i] = min coins for amount i. Try every coin: dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i-coin]+1)"},{"name":"Coin Change 2","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Count ways — change min to +=. Outer loop over coins (not amounts) for no duplicate combos"},{"name":"Climbing Stairs","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] — Fibonacci with 1 and 2 steps"},{"name":"Word Break","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"dp[i] = true if dp[i-len(word)] is true for any word ending at i"},{"name":"Perfect Squares","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Coin change variant where coins are perfect squares 1,4,9,16,..."}]

algorithms.name: Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS)
algorithms.analogy: Find the longest chain of increasing stepping stones across a river.
algorithms.story: Given stocks prices [3, 10, 2, 1, 20], the longest run of days where the price strictly increased is [1, 20] or [3, 10, 20] — length 3. This is LIS. The O(n²) DP solution builds dp[i] = length of LIS ending at index i. The O(n log n) patience sorting approach is elegant: maintain a 'tails' array where tails[k] is the smallest tail element of all increasing subsequences of length k+1. Binary search keeps it O(log n) per element.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Finding the longest non-decreasing/strictly increasing subsequence
• Chain of pairs: (a, b) is a valid chain if a < b — find max chain length
• Box stacking, envelope nesting, activity scheduling variants
• Any problem about the longest 'compatible' sequence
algorithms.howItWorks:
O(n log n) Patience Sorting approach:
1. Maintain 'tails' array — tails[i] = smallest tail of all IS of length i+1
2. For each element x:
   a. Binary search for the first index in tails where tails[idx] >= x
   b. If found: replace tails[idx] with x (smaller tail = more room to extend)
   c. If not found (x is largest): append x to tails, length increases
3. Return tails.length — that's the LIS length
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Start with O(n²) DP in an interview — it's easier to explain and code correctly
• Then mention O(n log n) patience sort as an optimisation — shows depth
• Binary search in patience sort: find first tails[i] >= x (lower_bound) — use carefully
• LIS length = minimum number of non-increasing subsequences to partition the array (Dilworth's theorem)
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Envelope Nesting / Box Stacking","desc":"Sort envelopes by width, then find LIS by height — directly reduces to LIS with O(n log n) solution."},{"title":"Version Control Merging","desc":"Finding the longest common subsequence (LCS) of two file histories relates to LIS on one sequence matched against another."},{"title":"Patience Card Game","desc":"The tails array in O(n log n) LIS simulates playing the patience solitaire card game — a beautiful algorithmic connection."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Confusing subsequence (non-contiguous) with subarray (contiguous) — LIS is subsequence","O(n log n) tails array is NOT the actual LIS — it gives the correct length but not the sequence","Off-by-one in binary search: finding first index >= x vs first index > x changes between strictly increasing and non-decreasing","Forgetting base case: dp[i] = 1 (each element is an IS of length 1 by itself)"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Longest Non-Decreasing Subsequence","desc":"Use nums[j] <= nums[i] instead of <; in patience sort, use lower_bound on > instead of >=."},{"name":"Longest Decreasing Subsequence","desc":"Reverse the array and apply LIS"},{"name":"Maximum Sum Increasing Subsequence","desc":"dp[i] = max sum of IS ending at i — use sum instead of count"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Know BOTH: O(n²) DP for interviews where you need to explain clearly, O(n log n) patience sorting for the follow-up. The patience sort tails array gives the CORRECT LENGTH but NOT the actual subsequence — you need the O(n²) DP with a prev[] array to reconstruct the actual sequence.
algorithms.patternSignal: longest increasing subsequence, chain of pairs, envelope nesting, box stacking, compatible sequence, longest non-decreasing, strictly increasing
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Longest Increasing Subsequence","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"O(n²) DP: dp[i]=max LIS ending at i. O(n log n): binary search to maintain tails array"},{"name":"Russian Doll Envelopes","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Sort by width ascending, then LIS by height — but sort equal widths by height DESCENDING to prevent using two with same width"},{"name":"Number of Longest Increasing Subsequences","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Track count[] alongside dp[] — increment count when finding equal length"},{"name":"Longest Increasing Path in Matrix","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"DFS with memoization — dp[i][j] = longest path starting at (i,j)"}]

algorithms.name: Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)
algorithms.spaceComplexity: O(m * n) · can reduce to O(min(m,n))
algorithms.analogy: Find the longest DNA strand shared between two genes, allowing gaps.
algorithms.story: Given two strings — 'ABCBDAB' and 'BDCABA' — the longest common subsequence is 'BCBA' or 'BCAB' — length 4. LCS is the backbone of diff tools (git diff), DNA alignment, and spell checkers. The 2D DP table dp[i][j] = LCS length of first i chars of s1 and first j chars of s2. The recurrence: if characters match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1; otherwise, dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Finding similarity between two strings/sequences
• Diff algorithm (git diff, file comparison)
• DNA sequence alignment
• Edit distance variants (LCS and edit distance are dual problems)
• Spell checker — measure how close two words are
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Create (m+1) x (n+1) dp table, initialise with 0
2. For i from 1 to m, j from 1 to n:
   If s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1
   Else: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
3. dp[m][n] = LCS length
4. To reconstruct: backtrack from dp[m][n] — if chars match, include; else go to larger of dp[i-1][j] or dp[i][j-1]
algorithms.interviewTips:
• LCS of length L means Edit Distance = m + n - 2L (insert all of s2 that aren't in LCS, delete all of s1 that aren't)
• Space can be reduced to O(min(m,n)) using two rolling rows — mention this optimisation
• Common follow-up: Shortest Common Supersequence = m + n - LCS(m,n)
• LCS is NOT the same as Longest Common Substring (contiguous vs non-contiguous)
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Git Diff","desc":"git diff computes the LCS of two file versions — lines in LCS are unchanged, removed lines are only in s1, added lines only in s2."},{"title":"DNA Sequence Alignment","desc":"Bioinformatics tools align two gene sequences by finding their LCS — gaps in the alignment correspond to insertions/deletions."},{"title":"Plagiarism Detection","desc":"Similarity score between two documents is often based on normalised LCS length."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Confusing LCS (subsequence, non-contiguous) with Longest Common Substring (contiguous)","Wrong recurrence: when characters match, use dp[i-1][j-1]+1, NOT max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])+1","Off-by-one: dp is (m+1)x(n+1) — index with i-1/j-1 for string chars vs i/j for dp table","Not handling space optimisation: full O(m*n) table may TLE or MLE for very long strings"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Shortest Common Supersequence","desc":"Length = m + n - LCS(m,n) — the shortest string containing both s1 and s2 as subsequences"},{"name":"Edit Distance (Levenshtein)","desc":"Number of insert/delete/replace operations to transform s1 into s2 — generalisation of LCS"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: LCS and Edit Distance are dual: edit_distance = m + n - 2*LCS. Table recurrence: match → dp[i-1][j-1]+1; no match → max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Space reducible to O(min(m,n)) using two rolling rows. LCS is NOT the same as Longest Common Substring (subsequence = non-contiguous, substring = contiguous).
algorithms.patternSignal: longest common subsequence, similarity between strings, diff algorithm, DNA alignment, non-contiguous common elements, git diff, edit distance connection
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Longest Common Subsequence","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"2D DP: match → dp[i-1][j-1]+1, no match → max(left, up)"},{"name":"Shortest Common Supersequence","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Build LCS table, then backtrack to reconstruct supersequence"},{"name":"Delete Operation for Two Strings","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Min deletions = m + n - 2*LCS(s1,s2)"},{"name":"Minimum ASCII Delete Sum for Two Strings","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Same as LCS but maximize sum of matching ASCII values instead of count"}]

algorithms.name: Edit Distance (Levenshtein)
algorithms.analogy: How many keystrokes (insert, delete, replace) to fix a typo from 'kitten' to 'sitting'?
algorithms.story: You type 'kitten' but meant 'sitting'. How many single-character edits are needed? kitten → sitten (replace k→s), sitten → sittin (replace e→i), sittin → sitting (insert g) — 3 operations. Edit distance underpins spell checkers, DNA diff, autocorrect, and fuzzy search. The DP table dp[i][j] = minimum edits to transform s1[0..i-1] into s2[0..j-1]. If characters match: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]; otherwise: min of insert, delete, replace.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Measuring string similarity (autocorrect, spell check, fuzzy matching)
• DNA/protein sequence alignment
• Detecting near-duplicate strings
• Natural language processing: word distance metrics
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Create (m+1) x (n+1) dp table
2. Base cases: dp[i][0] = i (delete i chars from s1), dp[0][j] = j (insert j chars)
3. For i from 1 to m, j from 1 to n:
   If s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] (no operation needed)
   Else: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(
     dp[i-1][j],   // delete from s1
     dp[i][j-1],   // insert into s1
     dp[i-1][j-1]  // replace in s1
   )
4. Return dp[m][n]
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Draw the DP table on the whiteboard — it shows immediate grasp of the problem
• Three operations: insert = dp[i][j-1]+1, delete = dp[i-1][j]+1, replace = dp[i-1][j-1]+1
• edit_distance(s1,s2) = m + n - 2 * LCS(s1,s2) — the LCS connection
• Damerau-Levenshtein also allows transpositions (swap two adjacent characters) — common follow-up
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Spell Check / Autocorrect","desc":"Autocorrect finds the dictionary word with minimum edit distance to the typed word — typically using a BK-tree for efficient lookup."},{"title":"Git Diff / Patch","desc":"Diff tools show minimum edit operations to transform one file version into another — edit distance on lines rather than characters."},{"title":"Fuzzy Search","desc":"Elasticsearch uses edit distance with a configurable 'fuzziness' to match search queries to index terms despite typos."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Wrong base case: dp[i][0] = i (cost to delete i chars) and dp[0][j] = j (cost to insert j chars)","Confusing the three operations: delete refers to deleting from s1, insert refers to inserting into s1","Using max instead of min: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(...), not max","Not handling empty string inputs — check m==0 or n==0 before the loops"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Damerau-Levenshtein Distance","desc":"Adds transposition as a 4th operation (swap adjacent chars) — edit distance between 'ab' and 'ba' is 1 not 2"},{"name":"Weighted Edit Distance","desc":"Different costs for each operation type — used in OCR where certain misreadings are more likely than others"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Three operations map to three DP cells: delete from s1 → dp[i-1][j]+1, insert into s1 → dp[i][j-1]+1, replace → dp[i-1][j-1]+1. Base cases MUST be correct: dp[i][0]=i (delete i chars), dp[0][j]=j (insert j chars). Drawing the table on a whiteboard is the clearest way to explain this in interviews.
algorithms.patternSignal: edit distance, Levenshtein, minimum operations to transform, spell check, fuzzy match, string similarity, autocorrect, one edit apart
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Edit Distance","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"3 operations: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1])"},{"name":"One Edit Distance","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Check if strings differ by exactly 1 insert, delete, or replace — no DP needed"},{"name":"Minimum Steps to Make Two Strings Anagrams","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Count character frequency differences — deletions needed = insertions needed"},{"name":"Decode Ways","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"DP with 1-digit and 2-digit decoding — related structure to string transformation"}]

algorithms.name: Subset Sum
algorithms.analogy: Can you pick a subset of gift prices that adds up to exactly your budget?
algorithms.story: You have items with weights [3, 34, 4, 12, 5, 2] and want to know if any subset sums to 9. Brute force checks 2^n subsets — exponential. DP uses a 2D boolean table: dp[i][j] = true if some subset of items[0..i-1] sums to exactly j. Either include item i (dp[i-1][j-items[i]]) or exclude it (dp[i-1][j]). This 0/1 knapsack pattern appears in partition problems, equal-sum splits, and target-sum counting.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Can a subset of items sum to a target value?
• Partition an array into two equal-sum halves
• Count number of subsets with a given sum
• Any 'include or exclude' decision on items with a capacity constraint
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Create boolean dp[n+1][sum+1], initialise dp[i][0] = true (empty subset = sum 0)
2. For i from 1 to n, j from 1 to sum:
   dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j]  (exclude item i)
   OR (if arr[i-1] <= j): dp[i][j] |= dp[i-1][j - arr[i-1]]  (include item i)
3. Return dp[n][sum]
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Subset sum is NP-Complete in general (large sums) — the DP solution is pseudo-polynomial, O(n*sum)
• 1D optimisation MUST iterate right-to-left — prevents reusing the same item in the same pass
• Partition Equal Subset Sum (LeetCode 416) is a direct reduction to subset sum of total/2
• Count of subsets with sum = target: change boolean to int, | to +
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Partition Equal Subset Sum","desc":"Can you split an array into two parts with equal sums? Directly reduces to: subset sum of total/2."},{"title":"Target Sum (LeetCode 494)","desc":"Assign + or - to each element to reach a target — reduces to subset sum after algebra."},{"title":"Package Delivery Optimisation","desc":"Can a set of packages fill a truck exactly to capacity? Binary subset sum on package weights."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["1D optimisation: iterating left-to-right in the inner loop means items can be reused (converts to unbounded knapsack)","Off-by-one: dp array size is target+1, index from 0 to target","Odd total early exit in partition: if sum is odd, no equal partition exists — saves O(n*sum) computation","Not initialising dp[0] = true — the empty subset achieves sum 0"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Partition Equal Subset Sum","desc":"Reduce to canAchieve(arr, totalSum/2) — LeetCode 416"},{"name":"Target Sum with +/-","desc":"Assign + or - signs to reach target T; reduces to subset sum with target = (sum + T) / 2"},{"name":"Count Subsets","desc":"Change boolean DP to integer DP to count all subsets summing to target"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: 1D optimization MUST iterate RIGHT-TO-LEFT to prevent using the same item twice (0/1 knapsack). Left-to-right = unbounded knapsack (coin change). dp[0]=true always (empty subset achieves sum 0). Partition Equal Subset = subset sum of total/2. If total is odd, immediately return false.
algorithms.patternSignal: subset with target sum, can partition, 0/1 knapsack, include or exclude, target sum with signs, subset count, boolean DP, equal partition
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Partition Equal Subset Sum","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Reduce to subset sum of total/2. If odd total, return false immediately"},{"name":"Target Sum","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Assign +/- to each element. Algebra reduces to subset sum of (sum+target)/2"},{"name":"Last Stone Weight II","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Minimize |S1-S2| = subset sum closest to total/2"},{"name":"Count of Subsets with Given Sum","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Change boolean to int, |= to += in the 1D DP"}]
